sentences.text: Yes.
When the Russians came in 1939, they did not touch anything.
They just-- they wanted water.
They went to the house, they found the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the owner.
They asked, may we have some water from you.
It was very polite.
Very neat.
No touching, no grabbing.
No-- They just went and asked permission for everything.
When the Germans came in 1941, you know, when they invaded-- they were polite, too.
They didn't grab.
The first troops were exemplary soldiers.
When the war-- when there were things were going on-- because I think it took them a week to get to our village from, you know, the Russian-Polish, Russian-German frontier.
Maybe five days.
So everybody was-- had-- had to hide.
No one took chances.
Now, I'm not talking about the Jews-- the general population.
The Czechs, the Ukrainians, the Jews-- everybody wanted to be in a shelter, because you don't know what's going to happen.
We went-- one of our neighbors had a-- a cellar made out of bricks.
That's strong isn't it.
But it was in the house, which was wooden.
If a shell fell into the house, goodbye all of you, because of the fire.
[LAUGHS] But the cellar was brick-lined with a brick floor.
The ceiling was wood, because it served as the floor for the rest of-- for the house.
We came home in the morning, and we come to our house, and it's full of German soldiers.
The trucks are in the backyard, and in the front yard, and in the street.
And we went in the house, and they looked at us.
And my father said, that's my house.
I live here.
OK, fine.
And they were-- whatever they were doing, some was sleeping, some-- just like the Russians.
Just like the Russians the second time in 1944.
And they were sitting at the desk, you know, with the maps open.
So one guy calls up my father, and he sits him down there.
And the-- they're not supposed to expose, you know, their maps with the markings on it, but who knew?
Who could read the map?
What does it mean for the little squares and triangles and all kinds of things?
So I guess he was saying.
And one of the-- of the soldiers, my father was sitting over here, and I was sitting next to him at the other edge and on the corner of the table.
And one of the-- the German soldiers that I was sitting next to him spoke Czech.
And he told my father-- and he asked him what he speaks, do you speak German?
My father did not speak German.
He spoke the Yiddish-- you could-- you could make some.
And he-- the German spoke Czech.
My father spoke some too, because of the Czech people live in our village.
And he asked my father-- and I could understand Czech better than my father.
And the Yiddish with the German, you know, mixed in, we had a conversation.
So the German soldier says-- don't forget these are the front line troops.
He says-- so finally finds out, I heard that you are Jewish.
Yes.
He says, you're Jewish, yeah?
Yeah.
He says, didn't you hear the way Germans treat Jews?
My father remembered.
My father said, I'm not afraid of Germans.
I'm-- said, I am an honest person.
I never hurt anybody.
You can ask anybody in the village that I've been a decent-- proper citizen.
I had no problems with anybody.
And the soldier says to him, that doesn't matter, you're still a Jew.
Why didn't run with the Russians?
Why didn't you retreat when the Russians were retreating?
Why didn't you go with them?
Me, I didn't do anybody any bad.
He said, doesn't matter.
That's with the first one.
Then later on, that's when the Germans-- you know, when the-- I think the second wave, you would call it, the backup troops came.
Those were the troublemakers.
But they didn't kill anybody.
They didn't harass anybody.
They only and we had a little storage room.
We had flour, beans, potatoes, you know, and everything dried.
They took that.
We had chickens running around in the backyard.
So you know, tried to-- you ever tried to chase a chicken on a farm?
Oh, you come for northern Michigan, right?
Then you know how-- how easy it is to catch a chicken running around in the backyard.
It's not.
You got boom boom.
Take out the gun and shoot them.
Pick them up and see, yeah-- went home.
They hopped up on the truck, and they went.
They didn't bother.
Actually, the Germans caused us no problems.
It's the natives.
And observing, you know, listening to the stories of survivors from all other places, you know, one Jew accusing the other that you collaborated with the Germans.
They had no way-- no choice, [INAUDIBLE].
The Germans issued a order to the mayor of the village, and the mayor had to carry out the orders.
When it came to liquidating the other Jews from the village.
The Germans issued an order and they said, whatever-- the mayor used to go every week to the-- to the-- you know, to the county seat, and they told him what to do.
Well, the Jews figured out, that this has to be a way out.
And they figured it out.
And they told the mayor, when you go over there to the county seat to discuss whatever you're discussing, don't say a thing about the Jews unless you're asked.
If they ask you about the Jews, you give them your honest answer.
But if they don't ask you, don't ask questions.
Don't bring up what you should do with the Jews in your village.
And you know something?
It worked magic.
Well, but I didn't know about this.
I knew, I learned about this in the '60s here in the United States from my mother.
Up till-- up till the middle '60s, I knew nothing about how it-- you know, how it was set up-- or how it worked.
Well, about a month, six weeks, into the German occupation, all the Jews were summoned-- all the men were summoned during the night.
There was not a German present in the village.
All the college students, because they were the guys-- the new leaders-- who collaborate with the Germans.
So the Germans told them, you bring us the Jews.
So they had horse and wagons, I think five of them, because we only had like 50 people.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they summoned all the men-- first you have to mobilize the community, right?
If you take away the men, what are the women going to do?
Fight you back?
No.
They got all the men in the middle of the night, some-- some time in the morning, and they told them, you come over there to the inn.
And they took them one by one.
Not two by two, or anything-- one by one.
The first one to go was my father because we lived closest to the inn.
We lived like five houses away from the inn.
And my father used tell me-- he didn't tell me directly, but when he used to talk with other men, because I didn't have enough sense so ask my father how-- what happened.
And he was the first one.
He knew all those guys.
Well, they got him in the middle, and they were going to beat the hell out of him.
And they did.
And he started hollering, boys!
What the hell are you guys doing?

You know, I-- Well, they became embarrassed because they used to come in mooch candies from him, you know, come to the store-- steal from their mother an egg to buy some candy, and you never turned them into their parents.
You know, what you going-- He probably understood people better than I ever will.
And he says, he got away the easiest, because they open up the trap door into the cellar, and the cellar steps were all the bricks-- you know the sharp corners-- he said he got away easy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you imagine what the other men?
So they called in-- the-- you know, the more men came in the vicious-- the more vicious they became.
Well, in the morning, the cellar had an outside little trap like a chute, because they used to chute the potatoes there and the sugar beets.
So if you were lucky and you got to that little opening, which was right next to the door that got into the room.
The room was ground level and the chute was right below the door.
And they got everybody in there.
You know, back, looking back, every time somebody came, got a bigger beating.
You know, broken noses, smashed up teeth, smashed up lips-- and when they threw them down the stairs-- you know, broken arms, broken legs, they were not actually broken, you know, they didn't dangle.
They were cracked, because those people became, you know, cripples for the rest of their life.
So we went to see, you know, what's going on.
All the kids went there.
So I'm not [? a next-- ?] you know, why shouldn't I go?
I went there.
And I went to that-- to that-- to that chute, you know, that potato chute.
My father said, tell your mother to contact the [INAUDIBLE].
The Baranchuks they were another branch of the intellectuals.
Whatever it meant, I ran home, and I told my mother.
I don't know if I even told her, maybe she was already on the road.
I came home, and I know my mother went and picked up her sister Eva.
Eva was the oldest daughter because all her brothers were there.
All the men were there except teenagers-- you know, 13, 14 they didn't touch.
But everybody about 13 or 14 ended up beaten up, bloodied up in the basement.
And the-- and my mother and Eva, her sister, they went somewheres.
Meantime the wagons were standing there.
You know, meantime, there were couriers going back and forth.
Not Jewish couriers, the other.
The women were summoned to come to that inn.
There were latrines because, you know, people used to have dances there, and beer nights, and all kinds of things.
First they hitched up a horse, and they smashed up the latrines.
The women had to empty out that-- the latrine or the pits.
Could you imagine for 10 years latrines sitting there?
A big accumulation.
And they threw rocks.
And the guys used to stand around-- these college students.
The men were already in the basement, so they were secure.
And they threw rocks.
You know, take a big slab of cement and throw it over and it splattered.
And they had so much fun.
Then they told all of the women to get brushes and pails.
They had to go home and bring up a brush and a pail or a broom.
And they smashed-- you know, the inn had rooms.
And they white washed them and they threw garbage and manure.
They had to clean them.
No soap, no-- they had to be sparkling clean.
And they used to-- they didn't-- you know, once you are on your floors-- your floors and cleaning, you got a kick once in the butt.
Because I went in.
You know, they didn't bother me.
And some of my little friends went in too and looked on.
But the most I remember is the latrine pits.
That was horrendous, terrible.
Meantime, my mother and Eva were gone.
Sometimes later in the afternoon, the-- the horses and the buggies were gone.
The women were let go home.
And next morning-- you know, you can only stay up so late.
We got no radio, no television-- you went to bed.
Sundown, you ate dinner, went to bed.
Maybe you stayed up another hour, big deal.
What's [INAUDIBLE]?
We're exhausted from our busy day.
Next day, my father was home.
Mmm.
So I heard that my father said that all the men were let go.
Years later I found out what had-- how that thing happened.
Remember I told you that the mayor was told not to mention Jews at the meetings?

Well, at the same time, when they were given orders to liquidate, to bring the Jews to the ghetto in Rovne, the women understood once you get into the ghetto you're a lost-- they already learned somehow.
You're in a ghetto, you're a lost cause.
You're a dead duck.
There's no out.
At the same time, the Ukrainians were told that they have to provide volunteer workers to work on the big estate, where the collective farm used to be.
But before the war it was a big polish estate.
Some absentee landlord.
And they had to-- you don't get paid for that, for working there.
So the Jews made a deal with the intellectuals.
We'll work on that estate for no pay, for free, as long as you don't ship us to-- into the ghetto.
And that's how the deal was start.
I don't know what else was involved, but that's how the deal was made.
And let's what saved 50% of us Jews in that village.
We stayed in the village.
We stayed in our homes.
We worked in the fields, but we never slept at home.
From about July or maybe August-- I don't know exactly.
Didn't take notes-- until September, October of '42.
We used to work on the farm over there.
No pay.
We came home before sundown.
We had dinner.
And soon as it got a little dark, we disappeared.
Not through the main streets, through the back-- back, you know, through the back door to the side streets, through the fields.
And that's all thanks to Mr.
Czepko.
Mr.
Czepko, the intellectual.
When we used to go home from the estate-- my father-- I used to hang out with my father.
And my brother used to be with my mother.
But my brother was a stubborn kid, little kid.
I don't want to do this, I don't want to do that.
They left him at home.
But my mother never slept at home, neither did my father, neither did I.
But on the way home for dinner to show, you know, that we're going home-- people had to see we're going home.
Mr.
Czepko used to stand by the-- byy his gate.
Called my mother and father and they used to talk, talk, talk, talk.
I'm hungry, I want to go to bed and they're talking.
Well, one day I sat down.
There was a lot-- was lots of logs.
I sat down, and I looked at Mr.
Czepko to listen what the heck they're talking about.
Mr.
Czepko was teaching my father the art of survival.
How do I know?
Because I went to college.
I finally learned, you know, what he said.
He used to tell my father what to do.
Then you become aware, you know, something is going on.
But how does it fit, you know, with me?
Later on, you learn that it was lessons in art of survival.
And in the army they teach you that-- special troops, or whatever.
He taught us-- he told my father, you never travel in the daytime.
If you're going from one place, you never got in the daytime.
If you have a hiding place, you don't keep your shoes on, because shoes are not good for keeping, you know, heat.
Your body releases heat, but shoes are very poor heat preservers-- insulators.
You take your shoes off and wrap your feet in rags.
You don't go on moonlight nights.
You have-- even when you go during the night-- dark nights-- you avoid places were lovers might be sitting around.
[INAUDIBLE].
You know, when you're a kid, what does it mean, you know?
What the heck?
Who's-- lovers, who's that?
You don't-- you only travel dark nights after 12:00.
That's when the farmers are definitely asleep.
Lovers or no lovers, they're gone because they have to get up early to start working with sunrise.
You only go to people that you trust, that you know they're not going to turn you in.
And probably all kinds of other things.
So my father told his brother [?
Lance, ?] and his own brother, and the other Jews, the other men, and all the women.
Some paid attention, and some thought he was crazy like a loon.
Where did you get those strange ideas?
Those that paid attention survived.
Those that didn't pay attention perished.
For they did not obey the rules of survival.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

And-- besides the cruelty of the natives, of these pseudo-intellectuals, these young college men-- if it wasn't for them, our village would have survived whether we paid attention to art of survival or not.
They were the people who were going to establish a state, who demanded freedom, who depended in independence, but had no respect for humans.
And it's also was confirmed to me in 1991.
And I had a guest here.
A grandson of the man who hid us for about six months, who sacrificed his life and his family to do us a good deed.
To-- to-- you know, to provide us with food and a little shelter.
And he came, he sat in my house, and he says to me-- he says to me, the Jews got what they deserved.
He's a guest in my house.
He's eating my food.
I brought him over here to show-- I told him, the only reason you're here is because I want to show your grandfather and your father a-- a sense of appreciation.
Because I can't do it for them because their-- your grandfather is dead and your father declined my-- my invitation because of his health.
So I took you.
You know, I sponsored you.
Otherwise, you would be gone.
But because of that.
So you see that in 40 years, 45 years, the things hasn't changed.
And it's very sad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed on the farm.
We worked on that thing.
We did all kinds-- my father finally got a steady job.
From the beginning, he got a steady job.
We herd the cows, the cows and the bulls.
We used to take them in the morning, we marched them to the river.
Because 400, 500 cows and there's no automation-- you can't-- you have to draw water from the well, you know, by hand, by a crank.
So we used to walk them to the river.
From the rive, we walked them to the fields to graze or to the forest.
In the evening the same thing.
We didn't have to milk them.
But we had to keep, you know, the barns clean, and have them fed and watered.
And we used to sit in the forest-- my father, and I, and a prisoner of war, a Russia soldier.
And we used to-- the cows could roam all over the place.
The Russian soldier kept, you know, sort of let them only graze a certain amount.
And we used to sit at the back, you know, to watch that they didn't go to the fields.
But we always sat on the hill of the forest to have command of the village.
To see if any-- if they're gonna kill the Jew, if they're going to come after you, maybe you can have spot them before they get there.
So we used to sit on the hill, you know, to-- to look out.
Then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then 1 September of '42, we were notified by the mayor that they're gonna come and liquidate us.
So we came home that day.
We came-- this all on-- on the QT.
How we found out, or what how it went, I don't know.
What-- you know, what-- But I do know we came home, we had dinner.
Whatever we had-- something to eat.
My father, my mother told me, you take with you.
Get dressed.
Put on all the clothes you can put on yourself.
Don't carry a bundle, because a bundle is a deterrent.
But I had a little bundle, you know, clothes.
I must have put on five or six shirts, you know, underpants, thermal underwear I had, couple other slacks, and a jacket-- you know, a warm jacket-- and a hat, and no gloves.
And something in the-- a couple shirts in the back.
And they told me-- now, this is already '42, I'm almost full 12 years old.
And they said, you know, you go.
You can go to this man, to Baranchuk and [?
Timosh ?] and the Baranchuk the other one, and-- and another person's name I don't remember.
And looking back now, these were all intellectuals.
All the people whose sons, or daughters, or some were cousins, were very politically active.
They were active too, but in a quiet way.
The other ones were gung-ho.
And I went to them.
OK?
They had brothers and sisters.
They are take-- they took-- so they went to all of these people to be sheltered.
And they took me in.
They didn't harm me.
They had food.
I had three meals a day.
I was hidden, you know, either in the basement or in the attic.
I was given blankets so if I lie on the straw, on the hay it wouldn't make any noise, you know, to keep quiet.
The dogs were always let outside to warn if there were strangers approaching.
My father-- my-- oh, I forgot to mention one important thing.
The village-- I don't know in what proportion-- there were those who were pro-Communist, pro-Russian nationalists, and pro-German.
This I went-- to-- for the hiring place for me, I went to all pro-German, pro-nationalists whose sons and daughters were actively fighting the freedom fighters.
The Communists were also fighting, but not on the Germans.
They lay low, because I guess they got instructions, I don't know.
And my father told me, if you run out of places where to hide, no one wants to take you in, you are to come to this and this man.
His name was Mr.
Klimchuk.
His house was in the-- like in the suburbs.
And he says, you're to go there on a dark night where there's no moon.
And if it rains, you go.
And you go cross country.
You don't go the main roads-- lessons from Mr.
Czepko.
And you go to the western side of the house.
There's a window.
You knock three times and a voice will tell you where I am-- what my father said.
Where he is, to let me know.
I'll be there.
This man will know, Mr.
Klimchuk.
Till-- till this day, I'm trying to figure out what kind of connection did my father have with this Mr.
Klimchuk.
I can't imagine, and I'm sorry that I don't know.
So, finally, I ran an out of places where to go.
I tried to venture on my own.
I almost got killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went-- hide-- hid in the forest.
I came through a couple with a newborn baby there in the forest.
And they were hungry, and they were cold.
You could see that.
They were almost naked, and had no clothes.
Clothes tears-- you know, gets destroyed in the forest.
So I took off a couple shirts for the baby.
Well, it was-- I waited till sundown, till evening, and I want to see Mr.
Klimchuk.
Let me tell you, when I got to that house, I was so happy.
I was sweating.
I was hot.
You know, walking-- and the-- the black-- black soil, you know, that sticks to your feet.
It's like walking with snowshoes.
All that mud cakes on.
I got there.
And he told me where my father is, in a barn about two blocks away from his house.
And I knocked on the door-- on the barn because it's made of boards.
No answer.
So I start trying to pull one of those board.
They're about 12 feet long.
And I found a board that came loose.
It was nailed down-- only one nail.
You know, so it moved back and forth.
And I got in there, and I hollered, Father, Father, Father, Father!
Ah, no answer.
All of the sudden, I fell into like a-- like a pit.
Guess who?
Right on top of my father, because my father and my brother were in a haystack like.
And they dug a hole, like a tunnel down, and then sideways, and they had like a pit there.
So somebody comes into the barn.
They won't know that we're there.
That somebody's there.
That's where I found them.
And that's where our really adventure started.
The barn belonged to a man that my father and my mother had a lawsuit against.
But I think they had mutual understanding.
They were still-- they trusted each other.
I guess it was an honest lawsuit.
But the man was a firebrand nationalist freedom fighter for the Ukrainians.
They used to-- they used to call every three or four days a-- a meeting of the people of the village.
And he used to stand there and orate.
You know, and scream and holler against Jews and Communists and Antichrists and all kinds of things.
Really loud mouth.
Really loud.
Everybody was scared of him.
Guess what?
A real pussycat when he found us there.
He found us, and he told us encouragement.
It was on Christmas Day-- on the Russian Christmas Day he found us.
That was his barn.
He had the right-- He came to get, you know, the hay to take back to the village.
This was his barn out in the fields.
And he told us that the Germans had defeated us at Stalingrad.
Do you know where Stalingrad was?
I didn't know.
That they were defeated at Tobruk, that they suffered at Alamein.
Ohh, such exotic names.
Where it was, who cared.
As long as they were defeated.
And he told us he's gonna go home and bring us food.
I think my parents almost died because the night before, my mother came there.
In the meantime, when I-- In the meantime, my uncle Chaim was there also.
So there was my father, and my brother, Uncle Chaim and my mother, and myself on Christmas-- on the Russian Christmas Day when he found us.
And he says, I'm gonna go home and bring you some hot food.
And I think they would have rather been in Siberia then wait for him with the food, because they thought this is the last day of our life.
